refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles agreed with customer). Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry and Tasking interfaces during simulated and live teleoperation scenarios. Document network conditions and loss rates; confirm ≤1% threshold is met or identify root causes for remediation.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands (Tasking) with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall receive equipment telemetry (Telemetry) with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss approaching or exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert interface.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of packet loss on operator control and equipment safety.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss ≤1%.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and packet scheduling to minimize loss of critical control and telemetry frames.
DP-C: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) and retransmission protocols at the application layer to recover lost packets within latency budget.
DP-D: Combine DP-A, DP-B, and DP-C in a layered architecture: redundancy for path diversity, QoS for prioritization, FEC/retransmission for recovery.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layer communication resilience architecture combining redundant network paths (DP-A), adaptive QoS prioritization (DP-B), and application-layer error recovery (DP-C) to achieve and maintain ≤1% packet loss under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: Redundancy logic (DP-A) must coordinate with QoS scheduler (DP-B) to avoid duplicate transmission on both paths.
FEC/retransmission (DP-C) must respect operator control latency budget; excessive retransmission delay could degrade safety.
Network Link interface must support dual-path routing; if single physical link, redundancy benefit is limited.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design that decouples concerns: path diversity (DP-A) handles network infrastructure failures, QoS (DP-B) handles congestion, FEC (DP-C) handles random loss. Coupling is manageable via clear interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) and state synchronization between layers. High safety criticality justifies the complexity.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, WiFi in specific zones, satellite uplink). If conditions vary widely, ≤1% target may be unachievable.
ASSUMPTION: Operator control latency budget (e.g., <500ms round-trip) is compatible with FEC/retransmission overhead. If latency is critical, FEC must use low-overhead codes.
RISK: Redundant paths may not be available in all deployment scenarios (e.g., remote sites with single ISP). DP-A effectiveness depends on infrastructure.
RISK: QoS prioritization (DP-B) requires network operator cooperation; if not available, benefit is limited.
RISK: FEC/retransmission (DP-C) adds computational overhead and may increase power consumption on mobile equipment.
RISK: High safety criticality means failure to meet ≤1% target could halt operations; robust monitoring and fallback procedures are essential.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) redundancy strategy (active-active vs. active-passive), (2) QoS priority levels for Tasking vs. Telemetry, (3) FEC code selection and retransmission timeout, (4) fallback behavior when packet loss exceeds threshold (e.g., operator alert, reduced autonomy, safe stop). Add a DP-E sub-requirement for continuous packet loss monitoring and SafetyAlert triggering.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication resilience logic for remote equipment operation, including redundant path management, QoS scheduling, error recovery, and packet loss monitoring. It interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) and depends on Network Link for dual-path connectivity.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands transmitted to Vehicle with ≤1% packet loss guarantee. Teleoperation module applies QoS prioritization and FEC to ensure critical commands (e.g., emergency stop) are delivered reliably.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Equipment state and sensor data received from Vehicle with ≤1% packet loss guarantee. Teleoperation module reconstructs telemetry using FEC and detects missing frames to alert operator.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Alerts Safety Services when packet loss approaches or exceeds threshold, or when communication quality degrades. Enables safety-critical decisions (e.g., operator override, autonomous safe stop).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Dual-path network connectivity (primary and backup) managed by Teleoperation module. Failover logic switches between paths based on packet loss metrics and link health.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss resilience logic. All communication quality mechanisms (redundancy, QoS, FEC, monitoring) are cohesive and belong in this module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary to Safety Services (intentional, safety-critical).

Vehicle module owns equipment control and telemetry generation, but not communication resilience. Assigning packet loss logic to Vehicle would scatter communication concerns across multiple modules and couple Vehicle to network infrastructure details. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but not communication resilience mechanisms. Assigning packet loss logic here would conflate communication quality with safety decision-making. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the resilience logic.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning packet loss logic here would bypass the Teleoperation module and create diffusion across multiple sub-systems. Not recommended; Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of packet loss resilience logic because: (1) it already owns the teleoperation communication stack, (2) redundancy, QoS, and FEC are cohesive concerns within teleoperation, (3) it has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert), (4) it minimizes diffusion and coupling to other modules.
containment_risks: RISK: If Network Link interface is not fully abstracted, Teleoperation module may become tightly coupled to specific network technologies (4G, 5G, WiFi, satellite). Mitigation: define Network Link as a technology-agnostic interface with clear contract for dual-path support.

RISK: If Vehicle module does not provide reliable Telemetry timestamps and sequence numbers, Teleoperation cannot accurately detect packet loss. Mitigation: establish clear contract for Telemetry frame format and metadata.

RISK: If Safety Services does not respond promptly to SafetyAlert, operator may not be aware of communication degradation. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert latency SLA and ensure operator HMI displays communication status in real-time.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the Network Link interface contract: specify dual-path support, failover latency, and packet loss metrics exposed to Teleoperation. Define Telemetry frame format to include sequence numbers and timestamps for loss detection. Establish SafetyAlert SLA for response time and operator notification.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layer resilience architecture) directly addresses the refined FR (≤1% packet loss under agreed network conditions). The combination of redundancy, QoS, and FEC provides multiple mechanisms to achieve the target. Assumptions and risks are documented and manageable. DP-D is technically sound and feasible.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must not diffuse packet loss logic to Vehicle or Safety Services. Clear interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) prevent unintended coupling.

Network Link interface must be abstracted to avoid tight coupling to specific network technologies. Teleoperation should not directly manage 4G/5G/WiFi/satellite details; it should work with a generic dual-path abstraction.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (95%) with minimal diffusion (5%). The only intentional cross-boundary interface is SafetyAlert to Safety Services, which is appropriate for safety-critical communication. All packet loss resilience logic (redundancy, QoS, FEC, monitoring) is contained within Teleoperation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of ≤1% packet loss is feasible via network simulation and live testing under agreed conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics (counters, rates) for measurement. SafetyAlert interface enables monitoring of communication degradation. Acceptance testing can be conducted in controlled network environments and customer-representative field conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss resilience; Vehicle provides Telemetry and accepts Tasking; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert. Interfaces are well-defined. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify redundancy strategy (active-active vs. active-passive), QoS priority levels, FEC code selection, and fallback behavior.
Define Network Link interface contract: dual-path support, failover latency, packet loss metrics.
Define Telemetry frame format: sequence numbers, timestamps, metadata for loss detection.
Establish SafetyAlert SLA: response time, operator notification latency.
Add sub-FR for continuous packet loss monitoring and threshold-based alerting.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-scoped and atomic at the system level, but the selected DP-D is composite and requires decomposition into sub-DPs (redundancy, QoS, FEC, monitoring). The assigned IM (Teleoperation) is appropriate and achieves high containment. To proceed, we must decompose DP-D into lower-level FRs and DPs for each resilience mechanism, and allocate them to sub-modules or design components within Teleoperation. This requires one or more additional decomposition levels.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands (Tasking) with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall receive equipment telemetry (Telemetry) with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss approaching or exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert interface.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of packet loss on operator control and equipment safety.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, WiFi in specific zones, satellite uplink).
Operator control latency budget (e.g., <500ms round-trip) is compatible with FEC/retransmission overhead.
Redundant network paths are available in deployment scenarios (or fallback to single-path operation with degraded performance).
Network operator provides QoS support or Teleoperation module can implement QoS at application layer.
Vehicle module provides reliable Telemetry with sequence numbers and timestamps.
Safety Services responds promptly to SafetyAlert and notifies operator of communication degradation.
open_risks: RISK: Redundant paths may not be available in all deployment scenarios. Mitigation: define fallback behavior (e.g., operator alert, reduced autonomy, safe stop).
RISK: QoS prioritization may not be available from network operator. Mitigation: implement application-layer QoS scheduling in Teleoperation module.
RISK: FEC/retransmission adds computational overhead and power consumption. Mitigation: optimize FEC code selection and retransmission timeout for latency budget.
RISK: High safety criticality means failure to meet ≤1% target could halt operations. Mitigation: robust monitoring, SafetyAlert triggering, and operator override procedures.
RISK: Network Link interface abstraction may be incomplete, leading to tight coupling to specific technologies. Mitigation: define clear contract for dual-path support and failover.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined network conditions and acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss threshold, latency budget, network types).
Network infrastructure assessment: availability of dual paths, QoS support, coverage maps.
Operator control latency requirements and safety-critical command response times.
Vehicle module telemetry format and reliability specifications.
Safety Services alert response time SLA and operator notification procedures.
Proof-of-concept or simulation results demonstrating ≤1% packet loss under representative conditions with DP-D architecture.
